lesson_title: Object sizes and the sizeof operator

cards.text: The {{c1::sizeof}} operator returns the size of a type or variable in {{c2::bytes}}.
cards.source: 4.3 - Object sizes and the sizeof operator

cards.back: Unary operator that returns the size of a type or object in bytes
cards.source: 4.3 - Object sizes and the sizeof operator

cards.question: What does this print (assuming 4-byte ints)?
cards.explanation: sizeof returns the size in bytes. On most modern systems, int is 4 bytes.
cards.source: 4.3 - Object sizes and the sizeof operator

cards.text: When using sizeof with a type name, use {{c1::parentheses}}: sizeof{{c2::(int)}}
cards.source: 4.3 - Object sizes and the sizeof operator

cards.front: What are the minimum guaranteed sizes for char, short, int, and long in C++?
cards.source: 4.3 - Object sizes and the sizeof operator

cards.front: Why do object sizes vary between systems in C++?
cards.back: C++ only guarantees minimum sizes to allow compilers to pick sizes that maximize performance for the target architecture. A 32-bit int may be faster on some systems, 64-bit on others.
cards.source: 4.3 - Object sizes and the sizeof operator

cards.text: The sizeof operator returns a value of type {{c1::std::size_t}}, which is an {{c2::unsigned}} integral type.
cards.source: 4.3 - Object sizes and the sizeof operator

cards.question: Are both sizeof calls valid?
cards.explanation: sizeof works with or without parentheses for variables (line 1), but requires parentheses for type names (line 2).
cards.source: 4.3 - Object sizes and the sizeof operator

cards.concept: Why you should not assume object sizes
cards.explanation:
Never assume object sizes because:
  • Sizes vary by compiler and architecture
  • Code may be ported to different systems
  • Can cause bugs when assumptions break
  • Use sizeof to get actual size at compile time
examples:
int x{};
int size = sizeof(x);  // good: use sizeof
cards.source: 4.3 - Object sizes and the sizeof operator

cards.front: What size relationships are guaranteed between integral types in C++?
cards.back:
Guaranteed relationships:
sizeof(char) <= sizeof(short) <= sizeof(int) <= sizeof(long) <= sizeof(long long)
cards.source: 4.3 - Object sizes and the sizeof operator
